preFix.title: Making a striped batt on a drum carder
preFix.subtitle: Layering different colours across the drum to create a self-striping gradient batt
preFix.excerpt: A striped batt is built by loading successive layers of differently coloured roving onto the drum carder before rolling off the finished batt. The stripes run parallel across the batt width, and spinning from the batt in thin cross-width strips produces a long-repeat self-striping yarn. The technique needs no additional equipment beyond the drum carder itself, and any number of colours can be used.
preFix.sourceNotes: The striped batt technique is documented across drum carder manufacturer guides and fibre arts guild resources. The cross-width strip spinning method for preserving stripe order is widely taught in guild workshops and documented in contemporary spinning publications.

content.text: A striped
batt
is made by loading the drum carder in distinct colour sections before rolling off the finished sheet. Because
carding

aligns fibres at 90 degrees to the drum direction, the stripes run across the short axis of the batt. When the batt is spun in cross-width strips, the spinner works through all the colours in order, producing a long-colour-repeat yarn.

The width of each stripe on the batt roughly corresponds to the length of the colour repeat in the finished yarn. Wider stripes on the batt produce longer colour runs; narrower stripes produce shorter repeats. This is not an exact calculation: the actual repeat length depends also on the thickness at which the batt strip is spun, but the proportional relationship holds.
What you need

items.name: Roving in 2-6 colours, any breed or blend
Soft tape measure (to check stripe width on the drum)
Hand carders (optional, for blending stripe edges)
Doffer or stiff brush for removing the finished batt

content.text: Step 1: Prepare the roving strips
Split each colour of
roving

lengthways into strips approximately 5-6 mm wide. These narrow strips feed onto the drum more evenly than wide sections and make the stripe edges cleaner. Lay them out in colour order beside the carder.
Step 2: Load the drum in colour sections

Turn the drum carder handle slowly and feed the first colour's strips onto the drum until you have covered the planned stripe width. For a three-colour batt with equal stripes, divide the drum surface into thirds: each colour occupies approximately a third of the drum's circumference.

When the first colour section is loaded, switch to the second colour without turning the carder off or clearing the drum. Continue feeding the second colour in the adjacent section. Repeat for each colour until the drum is fully covered.

Do not pass the fibre through multiple times at this stage: the goal is parallel stripes, not blended colours. A single pass onto the drum sets the stripe structure. The licker-in drum will transfer the fibres to the main drum in the correct section without significant lateral mixing, as long as you feed each colour in its own section.
Step 3: Optional edge softening

For hard, distinct stripe lines, stop after step 2. For a softer gradient transition at the stripe edges, make one slow additional half-pass of the drum: turn the handle about 180 degrees at low speed. This blends only the narrow boundary zone between colours and leaves the main body of each stripe intact. More than one additional pass begins to mix the full stripes.
Step 4: Removing the batt

Insert the doffer pick or a blunt darning needle along the join line on the drum (the line where the drum cloth begins) and lift the batt edge. Turn the drum slowly while lifting the batt free along its full width. The batt should come off in one sheet. Lay it flat with the stripes running across the short axis (left to right) in front of you.
Spinning the striped batt

Peel thin strips from across the width of the batt: each strip should include all colours in the stripe sequence, from one edge of the batt to the other. This is the same cross-width peel method used for gradient batts. Join the strips end-to-end in order: the colour sequence repeats with each new strip.
Common problems

items.fix: Feed each colour strictly within its planned section on the drum. Mark the drum's circumference with a small piece of tape to define the section boundaries before you begin.
items.cause: The roving strips were fed across the full drum width rather than in separate sections, or the drum was turned multiple times before the batt was removed.
items.problem: The colours are mixed rather than striped on the finished batt

items.fix: Weigh each colour portion before loading: equal stripe widths need approximately equal weights of fibre. Use the soft tape measure to check the width of each section on the drum before removing the batt.
items.cause: Uneven amounts of each colour were loaded onto the drum.
items.problem: The batt has thin patches in one colour section and thick patches in another

items.fix: Keep the doffer at the surface; slide it along the cloth edge rather than pushing it into the teeth. A slightly thicker batt (more fibre per section) holds together better during removal.
items.cause: The batt is too thin in places, or the doffer was pushed too far into the drum cloth rather than just lifting the edge.
items.problem: The batt tears when being removed from the drum
